Humans of the watershed. You know what this STINKY green mat is- cladophora. It is native to #GreatLakes but is impacting freshwater ecosystems, beaches and water activities. Why the overgrowth? 🌊🌊

Caleb Kowalski is a 7th grader at Put-In-Bay Middle School. When he needed a project for his school science fair, he turned to nearby Lake Erie for inspiration. He went on to win the Northwest Ohio Science and Engineering Fair for his project focusing on combatting algal blooms.

Key points: A gap-filling reconstruction of Lake Superior pCO₂ was trained on underway data. Annual lake-air CO₂ flux alternated between net efflux and influx over 2019–2023. Lake Superior's carbon budget demonstrated improved agreement of measured and modeled carbon fluxes.
Calling all 🍁 #greatlakes folks. I'm looking to add a couple #Cladophora Nuisance #algae sites to our ECCC Nearshore monitoring program. If you know a spot that looks like this, hit me up!

I have a kind of silly question about shrimp. Are there specific reasons why a wild fairy shrimp can be bright cherry red? Years back, there was heavy rainfall in a great lakes park, and I saw a shrimp that was so comically red that it was hypervisible in a muddy puddle. Is it just pop variation?

I was on the grassy shore of Lake Michigan and saw a bunch of yellow flowers growing in one sole spot. Curious, I walked over and saw a deer carcass that had provided the nutrients (phosphorus) for them.

The Lake Superior-type banded iron (which is found all around the world) was formed by ancient anaerobic microbes billions of years ago. They would release oxygen as waste, which would bind to iron in the ancient oceans. When the iron ran out, the microbes would die off, poisoned by the oxygen

Michigan, Ohio, and Ontario did not meet their shared goal to reduce phosphorus entering Lake Erie by 40% by 2025.
Most phosphorus comes from non-point sources, such as farm runoff in watersheds like the Maumee and River Raisin.

A new study from University of Waterloo water researchers suggests shoreline erosion is a far more significant source of phosphorus to Lake Erie than previously understood, potentially undermining expected gains from watershed management efforts. Read more in Water Canada: tinyurl.com/2nvzde45
